v1_7_2_response: Every launch-facing model mention now states that outputs are scaffold/model-generated indices, not observed outcomes or calibrated predictions.
Every launch-facing statement of uncapping is paired with scheduled prices, item rules, scope, audit, clinical governance, co-payment protections and place accountability.
Equity protections and a Māori/Pacific/equity review gate are treated as preconditions before recommendation-heavy posts.
status: Gate retained: review still required before recommendation-heavy posts

v1_7_2_response: RACMA remains excluded from active outreach; the pathway is public Substack, stakeholder validation, OIA/data requests and optional later engagement if RACMA approaches.
The 70 parameters are framed as a tiered scaffold with a small core load-bearing set, not 70 equally important claims.
PHO critique is narrowed to function versus intermediation, pass-through opacity, non-capitated funding and entry/transaction cost.
Current reform is now the explicit comparator; the claim is “necessary but possibly insufficient,” not “nothing is happening.”
Email-trail material is labelled as stakeholder intelligence or OIA target unless independently sourced.
status: Embedded: unverified items remain OIA/stakeholder targets

risk_or_counterargument: Capitation reweighting plus targets may be enough
v1_7_2_response: Posts and summaries now define what evidence would change the argument, including a successful current reform pathway.

risk_or_counterargument: Place-based commissioning may be more important than benefits
v1_7_2_response: The proposed architecture now treats place-based accountability as core, not optional.

risk_or_counterargument: Rural access may require public/salaried/infrastructure levers
v1_7_2_response: Decision-maker materials now state that scheduled FFS is not a full rural workforce policy and may need infrastructure, capital, housing and salaried models.
Co-payment protections are a gating requirement and a core parameter, not a downstream add-on.
Scope-enabled supply is conditioned on shared records, clinical governance, accountability and continuity.
The thesis is framed as upstream access being one plausible driver, not the sole cause of hospital growth.
ACC is framed only as a rules-based treatment-payment analogy, not a model to copy wholesale.
